# Run Report: only-lvmt-t09 — LVMT 1M Time Benchmark (16 Shards)

**Experiment ID:** only-lvmt-t09  
**Algorithm:** LVMT  
**Key count:** 1,000,000 (1m)  
**Variant:** time (no stat), 16 shards  
**Date:** 2026-04-15  
**Status:** CLEAN — 0 kills, 0 duplicates, 0 errors, 0 LOCK conflicts

---

## Environment

| Parameter       | Value                           |
|-----------------|---------------------------------|
| Machine         | fcs-0033                        |
| OS              | Linux (cgroup v2)               |
| Rust toolchain  | 1.67.0                          |
| Memory limit    | 8 GB (cgroup `/sys/fs/cgroup/lvmt`) |
| `drop_caches`   | 3 (before benchmark start)      |
| `CXXFLAGS`      | `-Wno-error -include cstdint`   |

## Commands

### Warmup (w05)

```bash
cargo run --release -- --no-stat --warmup-to ./warmup/v4 -k 1m -a lvmt --shards 16
```

| Metric            | Value              |
|-------------------|--------------------|
| Warmup epochs     | 20                 |
| Warmup depth      | 16 levels          |
| Warmup duration   | ~69 s              |
| Snapshot size     | 276 MB             |
| Snapshot path     | `./warmup/v4/LVMT16_1e6/` |

### Time Benchmark (t09)

```bash
echo $$ | sudo -n tee /sys/fs/cgroup/lvmt/cgroup.procs >/dev/null
cargo run --release -- \
  --max-time 5400 -a lvmt --no-stat --max-epoch 200 \
  --warmup-from ./warmup/v4 -k 1m --cache-size 2048 --shards 16 \
  > ./paper_experiment/osdi23/time_lvmt16_1m.log 2>&1
```

---

## Results Summary

| Metric                    | Value           |
|---------------------------|-----------------|
| Total epochs              | 200 (reported up to epoch 198) |
| Total wall time           | 181.6 s         |
| Avg time per epoch        | ~0.91 s         |
| Log lines                 | 119             |
| Data lines                | 99 (epochs 2–198, step 2) |
| Log path                  | `./paper_experiment/osdi23/time_lvmt16_1m.log` |
| Errors / panics           | 0               |
| Processes killed          | 0               |

---

## Throughput Progression

### Ramp-up Phase (epochs 2–40)

| Epoch | Time (s) | Ops/sec  | us/op  | Read Amp | Write Amp |
|------:|--------:|---------:|-------:|---------:|----------:|
|     2 |   3.411 |  58,636  | 17.054 |    2.562 |     1.681 |
|    10 |  13.121 |  87,692  | 11.404 |    1.689 |     1.665 |
|    20 |  23.762 |  98,009  | 10.203 |    1.683 |     1.681 |
|    30 |  33.708 | 103,179  |  9.692 |    1.671 |     1.670 |
|    40 |  43.330 | 104,306  |  9.587 |    1.693 |     1.693 |

**Observation:** Read amp drops from 2.562 → 1.67 over first 30 epochs.
Write amp high from the start (~1.68) — 16-shard overhead is substantial.

### Steady-State Phase (epochs 42–198)

| Metric          | Value                    |
|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Data points     | 79                       |
| Peak ops/sec    | 122,316 (epoch 164)     |
| Trough ops/sec  | 107,243 (epoch 44)      |
| Typical range   | 110,000–120,000         |
| Read amp        | 1.645–1.698             |
| Write amp       | 1.645–1.698             |
| Avg levels      | 1.669–1.677             |
| Access writes   | [4000–4300, 63000–68000, 0, 2] |
| Data writes     | 200,000 0 (constant)    |

**Selected steady-state data points:**

| Epoch | Time (s) | Ops/sec  | us/op  | Read Amp | Write Amp |
|------:|--------:|---------:|-------:|---------:|----------:|
|    50 |  52.570 | 108,738  |  9.196 |    1.666 |     1.666 |
|    60 |  61.353 | 115,206  |  8.680 |    1.678 |     1.678 |
|    80 |  78.992 | 112,344  |  8.901 |    1.650 |     1.650 |
|   100 |  96.943 | 110,741  |  9.030 |    1.668 |     1.668 |
|   120 | 114.350 | 118,574  |  8.433 |    1.661 |     1.661 |
|   140 | 131.323 | 120,499  |  8.299 |    1.647 |     1.647 |
|   160 | 148.770 | 110,845  |  9.022 |    1.673 |     1.673 |
|   180 | 165.962 | 114,074  |  8.766 |    1.670 |     1.670 |
|   198 | 181.600 | 115,596  |  8.651 |    1.665 |     1.665 |

---

## Key Observations

1. **Cache warmup takes ~30 epochs.** Read amp drops from 2.56 → 1.67 by
   epoch 30. Similar warmup speed to 64 shards.

2. **Steady-state throughput: ~115k ops/sec (8.7 us/op).** Significantly
   slower than both plain (177k) and 64-shard (153k).

3. **Write amplification: ~1.665.** Much higher than 64 shards (1.145) and
   plain (0.975). 16 shards means larger per-shard databases, leading to
   more AMT node writes per operation.

4. **Read amplification ≈ write amplification** at ~1.665. Consistent
   pattern across all LVMT variants.

5. **Very stable throughput.** Less variance than 64 shards — range
   107k–122k (±7%) vs 64-shard 119k–178k (±20%). Fewer shards means
   less cross-shard I/O interference.

6. **No burst phase.** Unlike plain LVMT (which shows a 56–68 epoch burst),
   16 shards has a smooth, flat throughput curve after warmup.

7. **Average tree levels: 1.67.** Identical across all variants — AMT
   structure is independent of shard count for 1M keys.

---

## Comparison: All 1M Shard Variants (Time Benchmarks)

| Metric              | Plain (t07)  | 64 Shards (t08) | 16 Shards (t09) |
|---------------------|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Steady ops/sec      | ~177k       | ~153k           | ~115k           |
| Peak ops/sec        | 219k        | 178k            | 122k            |
| Total time (200 ep) | 118.8 s     | 140.0 s         | 181.6 s         |
| Time per epoch      | 0.59 s      | 0.70 s          | 0.91 s          |
| Write amp           | 0.975       | 1.145           | 1.665           |
| Read amp            | 0.975       | 1.145           | 1.665           |
| Warmup snapshot     | 144 MB      | 177 MB          | 276 MB          |
| Throughput variance | ±8%         | ±20%            | ±7%             |

### Shard Count Impact Analysis

- **Plain → 64 shards:** −13.6% throughput, +17.4% write amp, +22.9% DB size
- **64 → 16 shards:** −24.8% throughput, +45.4% write amp, +55.9% DB size
- **Plain → 16 shards:** −35.0% throughput, +70.8% write amp, +91.7% DB size

**Key insight:** Fewer shards = worse performance at 1M keys. This is
counter-intuitive — one might expect fewer shards to mean less overhead.
But with LVMT, fewer shards means each shard's AMT is deeper/wider,
causing more node writes per update. The paper's choice of 64 shards
as the default shard count is validated by this data.
